meta.title: 9.3 Distributed Clocks and Ordering
meta.description: Logical clocks, vector clocks, Lamport timestamps, and why wall-clock time is unreliable in distributed systems.

blocks.prompt: Two servers each record an event at '14:23:05.123'. Did these events happen at the same time? Can you trust wall clocks across machines to be synchronized within milliseconds?

blocks.content:
## The clock problem

Wall clocks on different machines drift. NTP synchronization is accurate to ~10ms at best, often worse. In a distributed system, you cannot rely on wall-clock time to order events.

## Lamport timestamps

A logical clock that establishes a partial ordering. Each node maintains a counter. On local event: counter++. On send: attach counter. On receive: counter = max(local, received) + 1.

**Guarantee:** If event A happened before B (causal relationship), then timestamp(A) < timestamp(B). But if timestamp(A) < timestamp(B), A did NOT necessarily happen before B (concurrent events may have any ordering).

## Vector clocks

Each node maintains a vector of counters (one per node). A vector clock captures full causal history.

```
Node A: [A:2, B:1, C:0]
Node B: [A:1, B:3, C:1]
```

If all entries in V1 <= V2 and at least one is less, then V1 happened before V2. If neither dominates, the events are concurrent (potential conflict).

**Used by:** Amazon Dynamo (DynamoDB), Riak for conflict detection.

## Google's TrueTime

Google Spanner uses GPS and atomic clocks for globally synchronized time with bounded uncertainty: `[earliest, latest]`. Wait out the uncertainty window before committing. Achieves external consistency (linearizability) across datacenters at the cost of commit latency (~7ms).

Wall clocks are unreliable across machines. Lamport timestamps provide partial ordering (causal). Vector clocks detect concurrent events (for conflict resolution). Google TrueTime achieves global time synchronization with bounded uncertainty using specialized hardware.

blocks.prompt: Module 9.4 covers distributed transactions — what happens when you need atomicity across multiple machines.
